title: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects

pages.text:
MATH 464: LECTURE 2
RICE UNIVERSITY, SPRING 2020
1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
We alluded in class to a uniqueness property enjoyed by initial and terminal objects, whenever
they exist. Here it is. The proof of the following lemma is very simple, but it comes up over and
over again in all branches of Mathematics, so make sure you have really internalized it.
Lemma 1.1. Initial and terminal objects, whenever they exist, are unique up to unique isomor-
phism, i.e., any two objects that are initial (or terminal) are isomorphic to each other, and this
isomorphism itself is unique.
Proof. We’ll show the claim for initial objects. Suppose that P and Q are two initial objects in a
category C. The idea of the proof is to “play the objects against each other”.
Since P is initial, we know there is a unique morphism φ : P → Q. On the other hand, since Q
is initial we know there is a unique morphism ψ : Q → P . Consider the map φ ◦ ψ : Q → Q. Since
Q is initial we know there is only one map Q → Q, which must be φ ◦ ψ. On the other hand, we
know by the definition of a category that there is a morphism id : Q → Q. Thus, it must be the
Q
case that φ ◦ ψ = id . Similarly, we can show that ψ ◦ φ = id . Thus φ and ψ are isomorphisms,
Q P
and they are unique. (cid:3)
2. Maps between categories: Functors
It is conceivable that a category is itself an object of a larger category. If that’s the case, then
there would have to be a notion of “morphisms between categories.” Such a notion exists and is
called a functor. You have seen many functors already in your mathematical life: you just didn’t
know you were staring at them!
Definition 2.1. Let C and D be two categories. A (covariant) functor F : C → D is a rule that
assigns
• to an object A ∈ Ob(C), an object F (A) ∈ Ob(D), and
• to a morphism f : A → B of C, a morphism F (f ) : F (A) → F (B) in D.
[the functor is contravariant if it reverses the direction of a morphism instead of preserving it: to
a morphism f : A → B in C, it assigns a morphism F (f ) : F (B) → F (A).] This assignment must
satisfy the following properties:
(1) F (id ) = id , and
A F (A)
(2) F (g ◦ f ) = F (g) ◦ F (f ) if F is covariant, or F (g ◦ f ) = F (f ) ◦ F (g) if F is contravariant.
Date: January 15th, 2020.
1

Example 2.2. The forgetful functor U : Groups → Sets. Here U (G) = G, the latter being
considered as a set, i.e., we forget the group structure of G. We also have U (f : G → H) = f : G →
H, the latter being thought of as a map of sets instead of as a group homomorphism.
Example 2.3. The free functor F : Sets → Groups. Here F (S) is the free group generated by
the elements of S. A map F (S) → G is determined by where it sends the generators of F (S), i.e.,
the elements of S. Thus, a map S → T of sets gives rise to a map F (S) → F (T ) between free
groups.
Example 2.4 (For those students who have taken Math 444). Consider the category pt-Top
of pointed topological spaces. Here an object is a topological space X together with a marked
point x ∈ X. A map f : (X, x ) → (Y, y ) is a continuous map of topological spaces sending x
0 0 0 0
to y . The fundamental group is a functor π : pt-Top → Groups assigning π (X, x ) to the
0 1 1 0
pointed topological space (X, x ). A map f : (X, x ) → (Y, y ) induces a homomorphism of groups
0 0 0
π (X, x ) → π (Y, y ), so π is a covariant functor.
1 0 1 0 1
Example 2.5. The functor F : k-Vect → k-Vect where F sends a vector space to its dual space,
that is, F (V ) = Hom(V, k) = V ∗. This is a contravariant functor: you can check that a linear
transformation f : V → W gives rise to F (f ) : W ∗ → V ∗.
3. Ideal Theory beyond Math 463/563
We’re going to leave category theory aside for a bit. Please spend some time internalizing the
above definitions. Also: think of (and/or look up on the web) examples of initial/terminal objects,
as well as functors.
I’m going to assume you know a little bit about rings, ideals and modules from Math 463/563.
Please read Chapter 1 of Atiyah-Macdonald. I will only go over the material I think many of you
have not been exposed to.
3.1. Zorn’s Lemma and maximal ideals. As always, our rings will be commutative, with a
unit. Let A be a ring; recall that a proper ideal p ⊆ A is prime if A/p is an integral domain;
equivalently, if xy ∈ p and x ∈/ p, then y ∈ p. A proper ideal m ⊆ A is maximal if A/m is a field.
Equivalently, an m ideal is maximal if m ⊆ a =⇒ a = A for any ideal a of A, i.e., m is maximal
with respect to inclusion.
Maximal ideals are prime. Here’s a basic question: are we always guaranteed to have maximal
ideals? The answer is yes, but unless we put some restrictions on A, we’ll have to appeal to the
axiom of choice, in the form of Zorn’s lemma.
Recall that a partially ordered set S is a set with a relation ≤ which is reflexive (x ≤ x for all
x ∈ S), transitive (if x ≤ y and y ≤ z then x ≤ z) and with the property that, together, the
statements x ≤ y and y ≤ x imply that x = y. A subset T ⊆ S is a chain if for every pair of
elements x and y in T we have either x ≤ y or y ≤ x. An upper bound on a chain is an element
x ∈ S such that t ≤ x for every t ∈ T . Note that the upper bound itself need not be part of the
chain.
2

Axiom (Zorn’s Lemma). Let S be a non-empty partially ordered set. If every chain of S has an
upper bound, then S has at least one maximal element, i.e., an element s ∈ S such that if for some
x ∈ S we have s ≤ x, then s = x.
This statement is equivalent to the axiom of choice. If you are curious about this, take a look at
Paul Halmos’ book Naive Set Theory.
Theorem 3.1. Let A (cid:54)= 0 be a ring. Then A has at least one maximal ideal.
(cid:80) (cid:80)
Proof. Let be the set of all proper ideals of A, ordered by inclusion. Note that (cid:54)= ∅ since
(cid:80) (cid:83)
A (cid:54)= 0, by hypothesis. Let T = {a } be a chain in . Then a := a is an upper bound of T .
i i∈I i∈I i
(cid:80)
By Zorn’s Lemma, has a maximal element with respect to inclusion. This is a maximal ideal of
A. (cid:3)
Corollary 3.2. Let a ⊆ A be a proper ideal. Then A has a maximal ideal containing a.
Proof. Apply the theorem to the quotient ring A/a and recall that ideals of this quotient ring are
in one-to-one correspondence with ideals of A that contain a. (cid:3)
Remark 3.3. There exist rings with exactly one maximal ideal (e.g., fields, but this is kind of a silly
example). Such rings (A, m) are called local rings. The field k := A/m is called the residue field of
the local ring.
3.2. Nilradical and Jacobson radical. The Nilradical and the Jacobson radical of a ring A are
two ideals that come up often in algebra. They make an appearance in Nakayama’s Lemma, a
theorem at the heart of many proofs in Commutative Algebra.
Let N ⊆ A be the set of nilpotent elements of A, i.e,
N := {x ∈ A : xn = 0 for some n > 0}.
This set is in fact an ideal of A because
• if x ∈ N then ax ∈ N for every a ∈ A. This is obvious, but it uses the commutativity of A!
• if x and y are in N then so is x + y: say xn = ym = 0. Then (x + y)n+m−1 = 0.
We call N the nilradical of A. The quotient ring A/N has trivial nilradical.
Example 3.4. The ring Z has no nilpotent elements, other than 0, so N = (0). On the other hand,
Z/nZ has nilpotent elements. You can check that in this case N is a principal ideal, generated by
(cid:81)
the class of p, where p runs over the prime divisors of n.
p|n
